sections.heading: Definition and Epidemiology
sections.content: Peritoneal mesothelioma is a rare malignancy originating from the mesothelial cells that line the peritoneal cavity, the serosal membrane surrounding the abdominal organs. It accounts for approximately 15% of all mesotheliomas, with the remaining ~85% occurring in the pleura [1].

sections.heading: Disease Terminology and Anatomy
sections.content: Mesothelioma is now universally defined as malignant; the term 'malignant' is no longer used in classification [39]. The disease typically presents as a diffuse process involving the peritoneal surfaces, though extremely rare localized variants exist [15-18]. The anatomical boundaries are the entire peritoneal cavity, which includes the parietal and visceral peritoneum, omentum, and mesenteries.

sections.heading: Diagnosis and WHO Classification
sections.content: Diagnosis often requires distinguishing it from benign conditions like peritoneal inclusion cyst and well-differentiated papillary mesothelial tumor (WDPMT), as well as from metastatic carcinomas [1,44-46]. The World Health Organization (WHO) classification of thoracic tumors (2021) has updated terminology and introduced new entities such as mesothelioma in situ [1,4].

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation and Management
sections.content: Patients frequently present with abdominal symptoms including ascites (77%), pain (69%), distension, and an abdominal mass (30%), leading to a high symptom burden and often advanced disease at diagnosis [11,21,38]. Management requires a multidisciplinary approach due to the disease's complexity and rarity.
